9 hours ago

--

Before you understand Zone.js, change detection, or Signals, you need to understand how JavaScript decides what runs first.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Angular’s change detection is not magic.
It feels magical when you are starting out because you update a variable, an async call completes, or a button is clicked — and somehow the UI knows when to refresh.
But under the hood, a big part of that behavior depends on something much more fundamental: The JavaScript event loop.
Most developers including me had use the event loop every day without formally studying it. We write setTimeout, use async/await, call .then(), handle click events, debounce search inputs, and fetch data from APIs.
But then one day, something behaves strangely.
A Promise runs before a setTimeout.
A UI update appears earlier than expected.
A test becomes flaky.
A debounce does not behave the way you imagined.
Or in Angular, you start hearing words like Zone.js, change detection, patched APIs, microtasks, macrotasks, and Signals — and suddenly it feels like the framework is doing something hidden.
The truth is simpler: Angular builds on top of JavaScript’s async model.
So before we talk deeply about Angular change detection, Zone.js, or Signals, we need to understand the foundation they all sit on.
That foundation is the event loop.
JavaScript runs one thing at a time
JavaScript is single-threaded in the sense that your normal JavaScript code runs on one main call stack.
That means JavaScript does not execute two pieces of your code at the exact same time on the same stack.
When a function runs, it is placed on the call stack. When that function finishes, it is removed. The next function can then run.
This is why synchronous JavaScript is predictable.
console.log('A');
console.log('B');
console.log('C');The output is:
A
B
CNo surprise here.
But real applications are not only synchronous.
They wait for API calls.
They handle user clicks.
They run timers.
They update the DOM.
They respond to form input.
They animate things.
They receive data later.
If JavaScript simply waited for every slow operation to finish, the browser would freeze. You would click a button and nothing else would work until that operation completed. That is where the event loop comes in.
What problem does the event loop solve?
The event loop allows JavaScript to handle asynchronous work without blocking the main thread forever.
Think about a simple API call.
When you call fetch, JavaScript does not stop everything and wait for the server. The browser handles the network work outside the normal JavaScript call stack. When the response is ready, the related callback is scheduled to run later.
The same idea applies to timers, DOM events, and other asynchronous operations.
JavaScript does not wait inside the call stack. It schedules work. Then the event loop decides when that scheduled work is allowed to run. This is why understanding the event loop is not just a theory topic. It explains real behavior in real applications.
The three parts you must understand
To understand the event loop, focus on these three parts:

• The call stack

• The microtask queue

• The task queue, often called the macrotask queue
The call stack runs synchronous code.
The microtask queue handles things like Promise.then, catch, finally, and queueMicrotask.
The task queue handles things like setTimeout, setInterval, DOM events, and other host-environment callbacks.
The important rule is this:
After synchronous code finishes, JavaScript drains the microtask queue before moving to the next task.
This is why promises usually run before timers, even when the timer is written first.
A simple example
Look at this code:
console.log('1: sync');setTimeout(() => {
console.log('2: timeout');
}, 0);Promise.resolve().then(() => {
console.log('3: promise');
});console.log('4: sync');The output is:
1: sync
4: sync
3: promise
2: timeoutAt first, this feels confusing.
The setTimeout was written before the promise. It even has 0 milliseconds.
So why does it run last?
Because setTimeout does not mean “run immediately.” It means “schedule this callback as a future task.” Before that future task runs, JavaScript must finish the current synchronous code and then drain the microtask queue.
So the order becomes:
First, synchronous code runs.
console.log('1: sync');
console.log('4: sync');Then the microtask queue runs.
Promise.resolve().then(...)Then the next task runs.
setTimeout(...)That is why the final output is:
1
4
3
2This is not a race condition. This is the event loop doing exactly what it is designed to do.
Microtasks vs tasks
A good mental model is this:
Microtasks are urgent follow-up work that JavaScript wants to finish before moving on to the next larger task. Tasks are bigger scheduled events that come from the browser or host environment.
For example:
setTimeout(() => console.log('timeout'), 0);Promise.resolve().then(() => console.log('promise'));The promise callback is a microtask. The timeout callback is a task. So the promise runs first.
This matters because many modern JavaScript APIs are promise-based. When you use async/await, you are still working with promises under the hood.
That means the code after an await does not continue synchronously. It resumes later through the promise/microtask flow.
Why this matters in real applications
Imagine a banking dashboard.
A user clicks “Transfer Money.” Your app does three things:
It sends a request to the server.
It updates the balance after the response.
It also has a polling timer that checks the latest balance every 30 seconds.
Now suppose the polling timer was already scheduled.
A developer might expect the timer callback to run first because it was already waiting.
But if the transfer request resolves and queues promise continuation work, that microtask may run before the next timer task.
So the balance update may appear to “jump ahead” of the timer.
That is not necessarily a bug.
It is not always a race condition.
It is the queue order.
This is exactly the kind of thing that separates “I know JavaScript syntax” from “I can reason about JavaScript runtime behavior.”
Where Angular enters the picture
Angular applications are full of asynchronous events.
A user clicks a button.
An HTTP request completes.
A timer fires.
A form input changes.
A promise resolves.
A third-party library triggers an event.
Angular needs to know when something may have changed so it can update the view.
Traditionally, Angular used Zone.js to help with this.
Zone.js works by wrapping, or patching, many asynchronous browser APIs. When those async operations complete, Angular gets a signal that something may have changed. Angular can then schedule change detection.
This is why Angular developers hear statements like:
“Zone.js patches async APIs.”
That sentence sounds complicated, but it becomes easier once you understand the event loop.
Zone.js is not creating async behavior from scratch.
It is observing async behavior that already exists in JavaScript and the browser environment.
It watches things like timers, events, promises, and network-related async work so Angular can know when to check the UI.
Why Signals changed the conversation
Modern Angular is moving toward a more explicit reactivity model with Signals.
Signals make state changes more direct and trackable. Instead of relying only on the idea that “some async thing happened somewhere, so maybe the UI should be checked,” Signals help Angular understand exactly what state changed and what depends on it.
This is why Signals are such an important shift.
They are not just a new syntax for state.
They represent a cleaner direction for Angular’s reactivity model.
But even if Angular becomes more signal-driven and less dependent on Zone.js-style behavior, the event loop still matters.
Your app still uses promises.
Your app still uses timers.
Your app still handles events.
Your app still runs on JavaScript.
Signals improve how Angular tracks state, but they do not remove the need to understand JavaScript’s execution model.
Common mistakes developers make
The first common mistake is thinking setTimeout(fn, 0) runs immediately.
It does not.
It schedules a task. That task waits until synchronous code finishes and microtasks are drained.
The second mistake is thinking async/await blocks the whole thread.
It does not.
It pauses the current async function, but the rest of the event loop continues.
The third mistake is mixing timers and promises without thinking about queue order.
This can create confusing UI behavior, especially in dashboards, animations, polling systems, search inputs, and tests.
The fourth mistake is forgetting to clear timers and intervals.
If a timer holds a callback, and that callback closes over component data, it can keep references alive longer than expected.
The fifth mistake is debugging Angular change detection without asking a basic question:
“What async operation actually happened here?”
Once you ask that, Zone.js and change detection become easier to reason about.
A simple interview question
Here is a common interview-style example:
console.log('A');setTimeout(() => {
console.log('B');
}, 0);Promise.resolve().then(() => {
console.log('C');
});console.log('D');What will it print?
The answer is:
A
D
C
BWhy?
Because synchronous code runs first. Then the microtask queue runs. Then the next task runs.
So A and D print first.
Then the promise callback prints C.
Then the timeout callback prints B.
If you can explain this clearly, you are not just memorizing the event loop. You are starting to understand how JavaScript actually runs your application.
The key takeaway
The event loop is not an advanced topic reserved only for framework authors.
It is the reason your promises run before timers.
It is the reason setTimeout(0) is not immediate.
It is the reason async code does not freeze the browser.
And it is one of the foundations Angular depends on for change detection.
Before you deeply understand Zone.js, NgZone, change detection, or Signals, you need to understand how JavaScript schedules work.
Because Angular is not magic.
It is JavaScript, running on top of the event loop.
And once you understand that, a lot of Angular internals become much less mysterious.
